authorship_evidence: Project uses conventional Python tooling (ruff, pre-commit, bandit), consistent human commit history, and pre-dates widespread LLM code generation patterns. FIXME comments and TODO markers indicate iterative human development.

evidence.description: Server-Side Template Injection via Jinja2. User-controlled 'name' query parameter is interpolated into an f-string on line 38 to build 'content', which is then passed to Template(content).render() on line 41. An attacker can inject Jinja2 template syntax (e.g., ?name={{config.__class__.__init__.__globals__['os'].popen('id').read()}}) to achieve Remote Code Execution. The TODO in test_main.py lines 39-42 confirms this is an intentional vulnerability.

Reflected XSS via the 'name' query parameter. The user-supplied name value is directly interpolated into HTML content via f-string: content = f"

Hello, {name}!

..." with no HTML escaping. An attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript (e.g., ?name=). The response_class=HTMLResponse ensures the browser renders the injected content.

Reflected XSS via the public_ip value. The response from the external PUBLIC_IP_SERVICE_URL is embedded unescaped into the HTML content via f-string: content = f"...{public_ip}...". If the external service returns malicious content (or is attacker-controlled via SSRF/env manipulation), arbitrary JavaScript can be injected into the page.

Server-Side Request Forgery. The PUBLIC_IP_SERVICE_URL is loaded from an environment variable (config.py line 11) with no URL validation or allowlist. requests.get(config.PUBLIC_IP_SERVICE_URL) is called on every request to the root endpoint. If an attacker can influence this environment variable (e.g., via container orchestration misconfiguration or .env file manipulation), they can force the server to make requests to arbitrary internal or external URLs.

Hardcoded secret token. SUPER_SECRET_TOKEN = '5u93R53Cr3tT0k3n' is hardcoded in source code with a FIXME comment indicating it should be loaded from an environment variable. This token value is committed to version control and visible to anyone with repository access.

Debug mode enabled in production. FastAPI is initialized with debug=config.DEBUG, where DEBUG is controlled by an environment variable that defaults to False. When DEBUG is set to true, FastAPI will return detailed stack traces and error information to clients, potentially exposing internal paths, code structure, and sensitive data.

WAF rules are defined but disabled. The Coraza WAF configuration has 'SecRuleEngine Off' on line 13, which means all subsequent security rules (SSTI blocking rule 1001, SQL injection rule 1002, XSS rule 1003, directory traversal rule 1004) are completely inactive. This renders the WAF ineffective despite appearing to provide protection.

Hardcoded default name. SUPER_SECRET_NAME = 'John Ripper' is hardcoded instead of being loaded from an environment variable (the os.getenv call is commented out with FIXME). While the name itself is not a credential, the 'SUPER_SECRET' prefix and FIXME comment indicate it was intended to be secret configuration.

The 'name' query parameter on line 19 is a string default parameter for the endpoint. Although it comes from user input (query string), it is only used in string interpolation for HTML output — it is NOT used as a URL target for requests.get(). The SSRF risk is in config.PUBLIC_IP_SERVICE_URL (an environment variable), not in the user-supplied 'name' parameter. A scanner might incorrectly flag user input flowing to requests.get(), but the name parameter does not reach that sink.

The DEBUG configuration on line 9 safely defaults to False. The expression os.getenv('DEBUG', 'False').lower() in ('true', '1', 'yes', 'y') correctly parses the environment variable with a safe default. Debug mode is only enabled if explicitly set via environment variable — the default behavior is secure.
